caption: Figure 1: Non-resolving URL rates for DRBench models, grouped by provider. Each bar decomposes into hallucinated URLs (red; no Wayback Machine archive, indicating the URL likely never existed) and stale URLs (orange; archived but currently dead). Numbers above bars indicate total URLs per model.
description2: The figure is a bar chart comparing non-resolving URL rates for DRBench models grouped by provider, with red bars representing hallucinated URLs and orange bars representing stale URLs.
description3: A bar chart displays non-resolving URL rates, separated into hallucinated (red) and stale (orange) components, for several models from Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic.
questions: How do different models perform regarding the proportion of hallucinated versus stale URLs?
What is the general trend in the number of URLs associated with each provider?

caption: Figure 2: Non-resolving URL rates by academic field and model for ExpertQA. Fields are sorted by overall non-resolving URL rate.
description2: A line chart plots non-resolving URL rates by academic field and model, with fields ordered by their overall non-resolving URL rate.
description3: The heatmap displays non-resolving URL rates across academic fields, sorted by overall rate, for three models.
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Figure 3: Non-resolving URL rates by subfield within Healthcare/Medicine. The top 15 subfields (sorted by rate) range from 14.8% (Virologist) to 21.4% (General practitioner). Subfield labels A–O are keyed in the accompanying table.
description2: The figure plots a line chart comparing non-resolving URL rates for Healthcare/Medicine subfields, ranging from 14.8% to 21.4%.
description3: Four-panel bar graph displays non-resolving URL rates, ranging from 14.8% to 21.4%, across fifteen healthcare subfields (A–O).
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Figure 4: Distribution of urlhealth correction rounds per question (435 questions each, 3 models). The three models exhibit distinct self-correction profiles. Gemini 2.5 Pro (green) completes in 1–2 rounds every time: its two-phase architecture (Google Search grounding followed by a single verification turn) caps it at two rounds, and 44% of questions need only one. GPT-5.1 (orange) clusters at 2 rounds (61%), with 87% finishing in 2–3 rounds; the long tail to 6 rounds is rare (<2% of questions). Claude Sonnet 4.5 (blue) concentrates at 3–5 rounds (83% of questions, median 4), reflecting the cost of verifying its large citation sets (18.4 URLs/question vs. 9.7–11.1 for the other models). All three models achieve near-zero hallucinated citations in final output despite these different convergence patterns.
description2: The figure plots a line chart showing distinct self-correction profiles for three models, with Gemini 2.5 Pro completing rounds in 1-2 turns and GPT-5.1 clustering at 2 rounds.
description3: A bar chart displays the distribution of urlhealth correction rounds for three models, with rounds on the x-axis and percentage of questions on the y-axis.
questions: How do the models' architectures relate to their observed correction round distributions?
What differences exist in the typical number of correction rounds among the models?
